learning_outcomes.description: Possess a mix of theoretical and applied knowledge related to the current cyber threat landscape and the challenges confronting government, private & public sector responses to cyber crime offending in domestic and international contexts.
Understand and critically analyse the regulations, investigative techniques and responses associated with cyber crime offending against individuals.
Understand and critically analyse the regulations, investigative techniques and responses associated with cyber crime offending against the private sector / businesses.
Understand and critically analyse the regulations, investigative techniques and responses associated with cyber crime offending against the government / national agencies.

assessments.due_date:
31/08/2026 4:00 pm

The Short Answer Exercise will be invigilated in the classroom during the scheduled lecture time.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment will evaluate both your knowledge of course material (Mod 1 & Mod 2), and your ability to analyse, interpret, and critically think about issues associated with cyber regulations, investigations and responses. The questions will require synthesis of information, critical thinking, and application of material discussed during Mod 1 & Mod 2.

The Short Answer Exercise will be invigilated in the classroom during the scheduled lecture time. The assessment will consist of three short answer questions (each worth ten marks) and ten multiple choice / true or false questions (each worth one mark). Students will have 15 mins reading / planning time and 90 mins to complete the assessment. Hard copy of the questions and answer sheets will be provided and students will be required to complete the Exercise by hand. The Exercise is Open Book and students may refer to printed lecture notes, lecture slides and course readings. The use of a hardcopy Language Other Than English (LOTE) dictionary is also allowed.


The full task description and marking rubric for the exercise can be found on Blackboard//CRIM7090//Assessment//Short Answer Exercises.


AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The Exercise is to be submitted to the Course Coordinator / Assessment Supervisor in the classroom by 4pm on 31/08/26.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Students will select a contemporary cybercrime issue (e.g. ransomware, online child exploitation, cryptocurrency fraud, cyber-enabled disinformation, youth and violent extremism on social media) and write a 1000 word regulatory response brief. The brief should critically evaluate current Australian regulatory and criminal justice responses to the chosen issue and make two evidence-based recommendations for improvement.

This is a research exercise designed to assess student's substantive knowledge of course content and ability to present and develop a cogent argument. The brief should be written in formal academic style and in correct, grammatical, and succinct English.

The brief is restricted to 1000 words in length (excluding reference list) and utilise APA 7th citation protocol. Students must use a minimum of five academic / government sources found through their own research beyond the course reading list. Suitable sources include books, book chapters, credible journal articles and Australian government policy or regulatory documents. Students may use some tertiary sources in addition to the five academic / government sources if necessary. These may include online statistics from reputable sources, newspaper articles, etc. Please remember: lecture slides, Wikipedia, and university websites that offer theoretical definitions, etc. are NOT academic sources and should NOT be used as references.

The full task description and marking rubric for the exercise can be found on Blackboard//CRIM7090//Assessment//Regulatory Response Brief

Use of AI: this task has been designed to be challenging, authentic, and complex. Successful completion of this assessment will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. To pass, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. AI may be used only for simple tasks including spell-checking, grammar-checking, and translation. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
